Guide Progress 0%
Part 1 — Foundation

🌸 Arch Linux: The Pink Galaxy Guide

A Complete Developer & Security Environment — Built from Zero

Dell Inspiron 14 7420 2-in-1 • Service Tag 487C9S3 • BIOS 1.30.1 • Full Wipe • UEFI • No DE

Confirmed scope: Arch Linux Full Installation — Part 1 (Foundation)
Target system: Dell Inspiron 14 7420 2-in-1 (12th Gen Intel Alder Lake, M.2 PCIe NVMe SSD)
BIOS Version: 1.30.1 (HB.1.12.6) — Service Tag: 487C9S3
Current SATA Mode: ⚠ RAID On → Must change to AHCI/NVMe
Strategy: Dell anonymization → BIOS hardening → Wipe disk → Base install → Boot → Network → Users
Wi-Fi: FRITZ!Box Fon WLAN 7360 available (iwctl during install)
Hardware: Touchscreen ON, Camera, Bluetooth, Intel Wi-Fi, Intel VT + VT-d enabled
Mode: theory_code (layman + deep technical + commands)
Covers: Ch 0 (Dell Anonymization) + Ch 1–7 (Foundation)
Part 2: Dev tools, security, pink galaxy rice
💾 Notes & progress are saved INTO this file — portable across computers

Table of Contents

  • Chapter 0 — Dell Anonymization & BIOS Hardening ⚠️ DO THIS FIRST

  • 6.5 Bootloader — systemd-boot

  • 7.2 NetworkManager
  • 7.3 SSH Server

Chapter 0 — DO THIS FIRST

Dell Anonymization & BIOS Hardening

★ ★ ★ ★ ★
📝 Notes

I examined every page of your BIOS. Your Dell Inspiron 14 7420 2-in-1 has several Dell-specific services running that phone home, attempt cloud recovery, and track your system. For a clean, anonymous Arch Linux install, we need to strip all of this out before we even touch the disk. The Service Tag (487C9S3) is hardcoded in firmware and cannot be changed, but we can disable everything that uses it to communicate externally.

🚨 CRITICAL — STORAGE MODE IS WRONG

Your Storage page shows RAID On is selected. This is the single most important change — if you don't switch this to AHCI/NVMe, the Arch Linux installer will NOT see your M.2 PCIe SSD at all. The drive will appear to not exist.

0.1 CRITICAL: Change RAID On → AHCI/NVMe

Layman version: Right now, your Dell is pretending to be a server with a fancy disk array (RAID). It is not — it has one single SSD. This "RAID On" mode wraps your SSD in an Intel proprietary layer that Linux cannot talk to without a special driver (the VMD driver). Switching to AHCI/NVMe mode removes this wrapper and lets the SSD speak directly to the operating system using the standard NVMe protocol that every Linux kernel understands natively.

Technical detail: The Intel Volume Management Device (VMD) controller, when in RAID mode, enumerates the NVMe SSD behind a virtual PCIe root port. The SSD's NVMe controller becomes invisible to the standard nvme kernel module because the PCI device IDs are remapped through the VMD bus. While Linux does have a vmd kernel module, the Arch live ISO's initramfs may not autodetect it, and mixing VMD with a fresh install adds unnecessary complexity. Switching to AHCI/NVMe exposes the NVMe device directly on the root PCIe bus at its native BDF (Bus:Device:Function), where the kernel's nvme module immediately recognizes it as /dev/nvme0n1.

📍 EXACT STEPS (from your BIOS)

1. You are in BIOS Setup (F2). Click Storage in the left sidebar.

2. Under "SATA/NVMe Operation", you see three radio buttons: Disabled, AHCI/NVMe, RAID On.

3. Select AHCI/NVMe (the middle option).

4. A warning may appear saying this will affect boot — confirm YES (we are wiping everything anyway).

5. Verify M.2 PCIe SSD toggle stays ON (it is currently ON in your BIOS, good).

6. Click APPLY CHANGES at the bottom.

0.2 Disable Dell Telemetry & Recovery Systems

Layman version: Dell ships laptops with several systems that phone home, attempt automatic repairs, and connect to Dell's cloud. For a clean, anonymous Linux system, we disable all of these. None of them serve any purpose on an Arch Linux system — they are Windows-centric services baked into the firmware.

Navigate through each section in your BIOS and make these changes:

Update, Recovery page:

SettingCurrent StateChange ToWhy
UEFI Capsule Firmware UpdatesONKeep ONAllows fwupd on Linux to update BIOS — actually useful!
BIOS Recovery from Hard DriveONOFFStores a recovery BIOS image on your SSD — we are wiping the SSD
SupportAssist OS RecoveryONOFFDell's cloud OS recovery — phones home to Dell servers
BIOSConnectONOFFConnects to Dell's cloud for firmware/OS recovery — phones home
Dell Auto OS Recovery Threshold2OffAfter 2 failed boots, Dell tries to auto-recover — we don't want this
Connected Service DeliveryOFFKeep OFF ✓Already disabled — Dell telemetry service
BIOS Downgrade(check)Keep ONAllows rolling back BIOS if an update breaks things

Connection page:

SettingCurrent StateChange ToWhy
WLAN✓ EnabledKeep ON ✓We need Wi-Fi for install and daily use
Bluetooth✓ EnabledKeep ON ✓Needed for BT keyboard, mouse, headset
UEFI Network StackEnabledDisabledThis enables PXE/HTTP boot from network — security risk, not needed
HTTP(s) BootONOFFAllows booting OS from network — this is what's showing on your first screenshot. Disable it!
💡 EXPLANATION — HTTP(s) BOOT SCREEN

The first screenshot you sent (showing wireless networks and "HTTP(s) Boot") appears because your laptop is trying to boot from the network since it found no OS on the SSD. With HTTP Boot disabled and a USB stick inserted, it will boot from USB instead. That screen will disappear.

Pre-boot Behavior page:

SettingCurrent StateChange ToWhy
Adapter WarningsONKeep ONWarns if using underpowered charger — useful
Warnings and ErrorsPromptContinue on WarningsPrevents boot hangs on non-critical warnings
USB-C WarningsONOFFReduces unnecessary boot interruptions
Fastboot(check)MinimalFaster boot but still allows F2/F12 access

0.3 Security Cleanup & TPM

Your Security page shows Intel Platform Trust Technology (PTT) is ON. This is Intel's firmware-based TPM 2.0 implementation. For a security-focused Arch setup, we actually want to keep this enabled — TPM is useful for LUKS disk encryption, Secure Boot with custom keys, and storing SSH keys securely in hardware.

SettingCurrent StateChange ToWhy
Intel PTTONKeep ON ✓TPM 2.0 — useful for disk encryption and security
PPI Bypass for ClearOFFKeep OFF ✓Requires physical presence to clear TPM — good security
Clear (TPM)OFFToggle ON, then ApplyClears any previous Windows TPM data. Resets to clean state.
SMM Security MitigationOFFONProtects against SMM-based attacks — keep on for security

Passwords page:

Your Passwords page shows Admin Password, System Password, and M.2 PCIe SSD-0 password fields — all currently empty. For anonymity, do NOT set a BIOS admin password (it ties to Dell's database if you ever contact support). Leave all password fields blank.

System Management page:

Your Service Tag 487C9S3 is burned into firmware NVRAM and cannot be changed. The Asset Tag field is empty — leave it empty. Keep Wake on AC: OFF and Auto On Time: Disabled (both are already correct in your BIOS).

0.4 Recommended BIOS Settings Summary

Here is the complete settings matrix from your BIOS photos. Green = already correct, Orange = needs changing:

PageSettingStatusAction
StorageSATA/NVMe Operation⚠ RAID On→ AHCI/NVMe
StorageM.2 PCIe SSD✓ ONKeep
ConnectionUEFI Network Stack⚠ Enabled→ Disabled
ConnectionHTTP(s) Boot⚠ ON→ OFF
Update,RecoveryBIOS Recovery from HD⚠ ON→ OFF
Update,RecoverySupportAssist OS Recovery⚠ ON→ OFF
Update,RecoveryBIOSConnect⚠ ON→ OFF
Update,RecoveryAuto OS Recovery Threshold⚠ 2→ Off
SecurityClear TPM○ OFF→ ON (once, then Apply)
SecuritySMM Security Mitigation○ OFF→ ON
Pre-bootWarnings and Errors○ Prompt→ Continue on Warnings
VirtualizationIntel VT✓ ONKeep (needed for Docker/VMs)
VirtualizationVT-d✓ ONKeep (IOMMU for passthrough)
VirtualizationDMA Protection✓ ONKeep (security)
PerformanceMulti-Core / Atom Cores✓ All CoresKeep
PerformanceSpeedStep✓ ONKeep (power management)
PerformanceC-States✓ ONKeep (power saving)
PowerBattery Config✓ AdaptiveKeep (or switch to Custom 20-80 for longevity)
KeyboardFn Lock Mode✓ SecondaryKeep (F-keys are media by default, Fn+F1 = F1)
DisplayTouchscreen✓ ONKeep (useful for 2-in-1)
IntegratedCamera, Audio, Mic✓ All ONKeep
✅ AFTER ALL CHANGES

Click APPLY CHANGES at the bottom of the BIOS screen. The counter should show the number of changes made. Then click EXIT. The system will reboot. Now insert your Arch Linux USB stick and either press F12 during boot to select it from the one-time boot menu, or it will boot from USB automatically if the SSD is now empty.

System Logs: Your BIOS Event Log shows several "Battery is low" warnings and firmware updates dating back to July 2024. It also shows "Underpowered AC adapter" alert from January 2026. Make sure you are using the original Dell charger (65W USB-C) during the entire Arch installation. You can optionally select Clear Log on the System Logs page to wipe the BIOS event history for a cleaner start.

↑ Back to top
Chapter 1

Before You Begin — Tools & Prerequisites

★ ★ ★ ★ ★
📝 Notes

1.1 What You Need (Hardware & Downloads)

Layman version: Think of installing Arch Linux like building a house from raw materials instead of buying a prefab home. You need a blueprint (the ISO image), a delivery truck (a USB stick), and some temporary shelter (another computer or phone to read instructions). The Dell Inspiron is your plot of land, and we are going to flatten it completely before laying the first brick.

Technical detail: The Arch Linux installation medium is a live ISO image containing a minimal root filesystem loaded entirely into RAM via an initramfs archive. The kernel shipped with the ISO is a general-purpose x86_64 build that includes modules for virtually all storage controllers (AHCI, NVMe, USB mass storage), network interfaces (Intel e1000e, Realtek r8169, iwlwifi for Intel Wi-Fi), and input devices. When the UEFI firmware hands control to the bootloader embedded in the ISO (via the EFI System Partition on the USB), the EFISTUB or GRUB chainloads the kernel, which unpacks the initramfs into a tmpfs mount at root, runs the init scripts, and drops you into a zsh shell as root with a fully functional userspace including pacman, fdisk, and networking tools.

📋 CHECKLIST — WHAT TO GATHER

1. USB Drive — at least 2 GB (4+ GB recommended). This becomes your live environment. All data on it will be destroyed.

2. Another device — phone, tablet, or second laptop to read this guide while installing. You will not have a browser on your target machine.

3. Arch Linux ISO — download from archlinux.org/download. Always use the latest monthly release. Verify the SHA256 checksum and the PGP signature.

4. Ethernet cable (strongly recommended) — makes initial setup much simpler than Wi-Fi. If your Dell Inspiron lacks an Ethernet port, use a USB-to-Ethernet adapter or be ready to configure Wi-Fi via iwctl.

5. Power supply — keep the laptop plugged in throughout installation. A power loss mid-partitioning can leave you with an unbootable, corrupted partition table.

💡 TIP — VERIFY YOUR DOWNLOAD

After downloading the ISO, compare the SHA256 hash from the download page with the one you compute locally. On any existing Linux/macOS, run sha256sum archlinux-*.iso. On Windows, use certutil -hashfile archlinux-*.iso SHA256 in PowerShell. If the hashes do not match character for character, the file is corrupt or tampered — download it again from a different mirror.

1.2 Dell Inspiron 14 7420 2-in-1 — Your Hardware Profile

Layman version: Now that we have cleaned up the BIOS in Chapter 0, let's understand what hardware you are working with. The Dell Inspiron 14 7420 2-in-1 is a capable 12th-gen Intel Alder Lake machine with a hybrid CPU architecture (Performance cores + Efficiency cores), which Linux handles beautifully with the Intel Thread Director integration in kernel 5.18+.

Your hardware (from BIOS screenshots):

ComponentDetailLinux Support
ModelDell Inspiron 14 7420 2-in-1—
CPU12th Gen Intel Alder Lake (P+E cores, all enabled)Excellent — kernel 5.18+ has Intel Thread Director
GPUIntel Iris Xe (integrated)Excellent — i915 driver, Vulkan via ANV
StorageM.2 PCIe NVMe SSD (now in AHCI/NVMe mode)Excellent — native nvme module
Wi-FiIntel Wi-Fi (AX series, sees FRITZ!Box etc.)Excellent — iwlwifi module
BluetoothIntel Bluetooth (integrated with Wi-Fi)Good — btusb + intel-bt firmware
Display14" touchscreen (2-in-1 convertible)Good — Wayland compositors handle touch natively
CameraIntegrated webcam (enabled in BIOS)Good — UVC driver
AudioIntegrated (mic + speaker enabled)Good — PipeWire + sof-firmware
VirtualizationIntel VT + VT-d + DMA Protection (all ON)Full KVM/QEMU/Docker support
TPMIntel PTT (firmware TPM 2.0)Good — for LUKS, Secure Boot
💡 IMPORTANT — AUDIO FIRMWARE

Dell Inspiron 7420 uses Intel Sound Open Firmware (SOF) for audio. During pacstrap (Chapter 5), we will install sof-firmware to ensure audio works. Without it, the sound card will not be detected by PipeWire.

1.3 Creating a Bootable USB

Layman version: We need to "burn" the Arch Linux ISO onto the USB stick so the laptop can start from it. This is not the same as copying a file — we need to write the raw disk image byte by byte, so the USB looks like a proper boot device to the firmware.

Technical detail: The Arch ISO is a hybrid image: it contains both an El Torito boot catalog (for legacy BIOS booting from optical media) and a GPT-partitioned layout with an EFI System Partition containing a signed UEFI bootloader. Writing it with dd performs a raw block-level copy: the first 512 bytes include a protective MBR, followed by the GPT header at LBA 1, partition entries, and the actual filesystem data. The UEFI firmware reads the GPT, finds the ESP, and loads the \EFI\BOOT\BOOTx64.EFI application.

Method A — From Linux/macOS (recommended):

Preview — Writes the ISO raw to USB. Takes 1-5 minutes depending on USB speed. The USB becomes bootable.
ΔDT — Input: ISO file (readonly). Output: USB block device overwritten at sector level. USB loses all previous data.
bash 
# Step 1: Identify your USB device (DO NOT guess — wrong device = wiped hard drive)
lsblk

# Look for your USB — it will be something like /dev/sdb or /dev/sdc
# It should match your USB's size (e.g., 16G, 32G)
# NEVER use /dev/sda if that's your main drive

# Step 2: Unmount any auto-mounted partitions
sudo umount /dev/sdX*   # Replace X with your letter

# Step 3: Write the ISO — THIS DESTROYS ALL DATA ON THE USB
sudo dd bs=4M if=archlinux-2025.03.01-x86_64.iso of=/dev/sdX conv=fsync oflag=direct status=progress
Expected Output
986+0 records in
986+0 records out
1032585216 bytes (1.0 GB, 985 MiB) copied, 42.3 s, 24.4 MB/s

The bs=4M flag sets the block size to 4 megabytes, which dramatically speeds up the copy compared to the default 512 bytes (dd would otherwise make thousands of tiny system calls). The conv=fsync ensures all data is flushed from kernel buffers to the physical USB device before dd reports completion — without this, dd might finish while data is still sitting in the kernel's page cache. The oflag=direct bypasses the page cache entirely for writes, ensuring data goes straight to the device driver. The status=progress flag shows a live progress counter so you know the operation is not frozen.

Method B — From Windows (using Rufus):

Download Rufus from rufus.ie. Select your USB device, choose the Arch ISO file, set partition scheme to GPT, target system to UEFI (non-CSM), and click Start. When prompted for write mode, select DD Image mode (not ISO mode). Rufus will write the image identically to how dd works — sector by sector — which preserves the hybrid layout that UEFI expects.

↑ Back to top
Chapter 2

Secure Disk Wiping

★ ★ ★ ★ ★
📝 Notes

2.1 Why Wiping Matters

Layman version: When you "delete" a file normally, the computer does not actually erase the data — it just removes the label from the filing cabinet drawer and marks the space as "available." The actual papers (data) remain until something new is written on top. A secure wipe shreds every single page, so nothing can be recovered — not even by forensic tools.

Technical detail: Modern filesystems (NTFS, ext4, btrfs) use metadata structures (MFT records, inodes, B-trees) that point to data block locations. Deleting a file removes or marks the metadata entry as free, but the actual data sectors on the storage medium remain untouched. Forensic tools like photorec, testdisk, or autopsy can scan raw sectors and reconstruct files from their magic bytes and structural patterns.

For SSDs specifically, the situation is more complex because of the Flash Translation Layer (FTL). An SSD's controller maintains a mapping table between Logical Block Addresses (LBAs) the OS sees and Physical Page Addresses (PPAs) on the NAND flash chips. When you write to an LBA, the FTL may map it to a completely different physical page (due to wear leveling), leaving the old data on the original page until garbage collection reclaims it. This means even overwriting every LBA once may not touch every physical NAND page — some stale pages may remain in the over-provisioned area. For thorough erasure on SSDs, the controller-level Secure Erase or NVMe Sanitize command is ideal because it instructs the firmware to reset its internal mapping table and (optionally) cryptographically erase the data encryption key, rendering all stored data unrecoverable regardless of physical page state.

2.2 Methods: dd, shred, blkdiscard, NVMe Sanitize

You said you already cleaned the SSD but are not 100% sure. Here are the methods, from simplest to most thorough. You are running these from the Arch live USB environment (after booting from USB).

⚠️ CRITICAL — IDENTIFY THE CORRECT DRIVE

Run lsblk first. Your internal SSD is likely /dev/nvme0n1 (NVMe) or /dev/sda (SATA). Your USB stick is a different device. Triple-check before running any wipe command — there is zero undo.

Method A — blkdiscard (fastest for SSDs, 1–30 seconds)

Preview — Sends TRIM/UNMAP commands to every sector. The SSD controller marks all blocks as empty. Nearly instant.
ΔDT — Before: SSD contains old data (partition tables, filesystems, user files). After: all LBAs return zeros; FTL mapping table reset for trimmed ranges.
bash 
# blkdiscard issues TRIM to the entire device
# Fast and effective for SSDs, but does NOT overwrite NAND cells —
# it tells the FTL those LBAs are free. Data on physical pages
# may linger until garbage collection runs.

blkdiscard --force /dev/nvme0n1
Expected Output
(no output on success — returns to prompt in seconds)

The blkdiscard command sends ATA TRIM (for SATA SSDs) or NVMe Deallocate (for NVMe drives) commands for every logical block on the device. The SSD controller then knows it can reclaim those physical pages during garbage collection. Most controllers will immediately start returning zeros for trimmed LBAs (deterministic TRIM), but the actual NAND cells may retain data until physically erased and reused. For a clean Arch install this is perfectly sufficient — we are not protecting nuclear secrets, just ensuring a clean slate. However, if you want forensic-level erasure, use Method C or D.

Method B — dd with zeros (moderate, 5–30 min depending on SSD size)

Preview — Overwrites every byte with zero. Slower but guarantees all LBAs contain zeros.
bash 
# Write zeros to every logical block address
dd if=/dev/zero of=/dev/nvme0n1 bs=4M status=progress

# Optionally, also write random data (slower but defeats pattern analysis)
dd if=/dev/urandom of=/dev/nvme0n1 bs=4M status=progress
Expected Output
dd: error writing '/dev/nvme0n1': No space left on device
57345+0 records in
57344+0 records out
240057409536 bytes (240 GB, 224 GiB) copied, 186.2 s, 1.3 GB/s

The "No space left on device" message at the end is expected and correct — it means dd wrote zeros until it hit the end of the device, which is exactly what we want. The /dev/zero is a virtual device that produces an infinite stream of null bytes (0x00). Every read system call to it fills the buffer with zeros, which dd then writes to the SSD. Note that due to the FTL's wear leveling, some physical NAND pages in the over-provisioned area might not be touched, but all LBAs accessible to the OS will be zeroed.

Method C — NVMe Secure Erase (most thorough, seconds)

Preview — Uses the NVMe controller's built-in Format command to cryptographically erase the drive at firmware level. Reaches over-provisioned areas that dd cannot.
bash 
# First check if your NVMe drive supports format/sanitize
nvme id-ctrl /dev/nvme0n1 -H | grep "Format NVM"

# If "Format NVM Supported" shows yes, run the format command
# --ses=1 means Secure Erase Setting = User Data Erase
# --ses=2 means Cryptographic Erase (reset encryption key)
nvme format /dev/nvme0n1 --ses=1

# For the most paranoid level (crypto erase, if supported):
nvme format /dev/nvme0n1 --ses=2
Expected Output
Success formatting namespace:0xffffffff

The NVMe Format command is the gold standard for SSD erasure. When you send --ses=1 (User Data Erase), the controller's firmware performs an internal erase of all NAND blocks — including the over-provisioned area, spare blocks, and any data cached in the SSD's DRAM write buffer. With --ses=2 (Cryptographic Erase), the controller generates a new encryption key for its internal AES engine — since all data on the NAND was encrypted with the old key, it becomes permanently unreadable without actually touching each NAND cell. This takes milliseconds because no physical erasing occurs — only the key is destroyed.

2.3 Verification

bash 
# Read the first 1MB and check it's all zeros
dd if=/dev/nvme0n1 bs=1M count=1 | hexdump -C | head -20

# All lines should show 00 00 00 00… — if you see any other data,
# the wipe was incomplete

# Verify the partition table is gone
fdisk -l /dev/nvme0n1
Expected Output
00000000  00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00  00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00  |................|
*
00100000

Disk /dev/nvme0n1: 238.47 GiB, 256060514304 bytes, 500118192 sectors
Disk model: PC SN530 NVMe WDC 256GB
(no partition table found)

The * in hexdump output is a compaction marker — it means "all lines between the previous and next are identical." So seeing 00000000 then * then 00100000 means every byte from offset 0 to 0x100000 (1 MB) is zero. The fdisk -l confirming "no partition table found" means the GPT/MBR structures have been wiped. Your SSD is now a completely blank slate — a tabula rasa ready for Arch Linux.

↑ Back to top
Chapter 3

Booting the Live Environment

★ ★ ★ ★ ★
📝 Notes

3.1 USB Boot & UEFI Verification

Layman version: Insert the USB, power on the Dell. It should automatically boot from the USB (because we set the boot order in BIOS). You will see the Arch Linux boot menu. Select the first entry and press Enter. After a few seconds of text scrolling, you will be dropped into a command line as the root user. No graphical interface — just a blinking cursor waiting for your instructions. This is the Arch live environment: a temporary mini-operating-system running entirely in RAM from the USB.

Technical detail: The UEFI firmware reads the GPT from the USB, finds the EFI System Partition (partition type GUID C12A7328-F81F-11D2-BA4B-00A0C93EC93B), locates the bootloader at \EFI\BOOT\BOOTx64.EFI, loads it into memory, and transfers execution. This bootloader (either systemd-boot or GRUB, depending on the ISO) presents a menu. Selecting "Arch Linux install medium" loads the kernel (vmlinuz-linux) and the initramfs (initramfs-linux.img) into memory. The kernel decompresses, initializes the CPU (sets up page tables, enables paging, configures the GDT/IDT), discovers hardware via ACPI/DMI tables, loads driver modules, mounts the initramfs as a tmpfs root, and runs the init process which eventually gives you a root shell.

After the boot menu appears and you land at a prompt, verify you booted in UEFI mode (not legacy BIOS):

bash 
# This directory exists ONLY when booted in UEFI mode
ls /sys/firmware/efi/efivars
Expected Output
Boot0000-8be4df61-... BootCurrent-8be4df61-... BootOrder-8be4df61-...
(many EFI variable files listed)

If you see a list of files, you are in UEFI mode. If you get "No such file or directory," you accidentally booted in legacy BIOS mode — reboot, enter BIOS settings (F2), disable CSM/Legacy boot, and try again. Running in UEFI mode is essential because we will use a GPT partition table with an EFI System Partition and systemd-boot, which requires UEFI.

3.2 Keyboard, Font & Clock

bash 
# Default keyboard layout is US. For German layout:
loadkeys de-latin1

# For a larger, more readable console font:
setfont ter-128b

# Synchronize the system clock via NTP
timedatectl set-ntp true

# Verify time is correct
timedatectl status
Expected Output
               Local time: Sat 2026-03-15 14:23:45 UTC
           Universal time: Sat 2026-03-15 14:23:45 UTC
                 RTC time: Sat 2026-03-15 14:23:45
                Time zone: UTC (UTC, +0000)
System clock synchronized: yes
              NTP service: active
          RTC in local TZ: no

The loadkeys command changes the kernel's keyboard translation table in the virtual console. It remaps scancodes (the raw electrical signals from key presses) to keycodes and then to characters. The de-latin1 keymap swaps Y/Z, adds umlauts on their expected keys, and remaps special characters for a German keyboard layout. The timedatectl set-ntp true command starts systemd-timesyncd, which queries NTP servers (pool.ntp.org by default) to synchronize the system clock. Accurate time is important because package signing verification and HTTPS certificate validation depend on the system clock being reasonably correct.

3.3 Internet Connection (Ethernet & Wi-Fi)

Layman version: We need the internet to download all the packages that make up Arch Linux. If you plugged in an Ethernet cable, you probably already have a connection. If you need Wi-Fi, we will use a tool called iwctl to scan for networks and connect.

Ethernet (usually automatic):

bash 
# Test internet connectivity
ping -c 3 archlinux.org
Expected Output
PING archlinux.org (95.217.163.246) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from archlinux.org: icmp_seq=1 ttl=51 time=28.4 ms
64 bytes from archlinux.org: icmp_seq=2 ttl=51 time=27.9 ms
64 bytes from archlinux.org: icmp_seq=3 ttl=51 time=28.1 ms

--- archlinux.org ping statistics ---
3 packets transmitted, 3 received, 0% packet loss

If you see responses, you are connected. If not, check that the Ethernet cable is plugged in and try dhcpcd to request an IP address via DHCP.

Wi-Fi (using iwctl):

bash 
# Enter the iwd interactive shell
iwctl

# Inside iwctl: list your wireless devices
device list

# Scan for networks (replace wlan0 with your device name)
station wlan0 scan
station wlan0 get-networks

# Connect to your network (it will prompt for password)
station wlan0 connect "YourNetworkName"

# Exit iwctl
exit

# Verify connection
ping -c 3 archlinux.org
Expected Output (inside iwctl)
                     Devices
----------------------------------------------
  Name    Address             Powered   Mode
----------------------------------------------
  wlan0   xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx   on        station

          Available Networks
----------------------------------------------
      Network Name          Security    Signal
----------------------------------------------
  >   YourNetworkName       psk         ****
      NeighborWifi          psk         **

Technical detail: iwd (iNet Wireless Daemon) communicates with the kernel's cfg80211 and mac80211 subsystems via netlink sockets. When you run station wlan0 scan, iwd instructs the wireless driver (e.g., iwlwifi for Intel Wi-Fi) to enter active scanning mode: the radio cycles through each supported channel (2.4 GHz channels 1-14 and 5 GHz channels), sends Probe Request frames, and listens for Beacon and Probe Response frames from access points. Each response contains the SSID, BSSID, supported data rates, RSN (security) capabilities, and signal strength (RSSI). When you connect, iwd performs the WPA2 4-way handshake: it derives the Pairwise Master Key (PMK) from your passphrase and the SSID using PBKDF2-SHA1 (4096 iterations), then exchanges nonces with the AP to derive the Pairwise Transient Key (PTK) for encrypting unicast traffic. Once the handshake completes, iwd requests an IP address via DHCP.

↑ Back to top
Chapter 4

Disk Partitioning & Filesystems

★ ★ ★ ★ ★
📝 Notes

4.1 Partition Layout Strategy

Layman version: Think of partitioning like dividing an empty plot of land into zones: a small zone for the garage (boot), a zone for the toolshed (swap), a zone for the main house (root/system), and a zone for your personal belongings (home). Each zone gets its own fence (partition) and its own organizational system (filesystem).

Technical detail: We will use a GPT (GUID Partition Table) layout, which is required for UEFI booting and supports drives larger than 2 TB. GPT stores partition metadata in the first and last sectors of the disk (primary and backup GPT headers), with CRC32 checksums for integrity verification. Each partition entry contains a type GUID, a unique partition GUID, start/end LBAs, a name, and attribute flags.

PartitionMount PointSizeTypeFilesystemPurpose
1 — EFI/boot512 MiBEFI SystemFAT32Bootloader, kernels, initramfs
2 — Swap[swap]8 GiBLinux swapswapVirtual memory, hibernation
3 — Root/60 GiBLinux filesystemext4OS, packages, configs
4 — Home/homeRemainderLinux filesystemext4Personal files, projects, dotfiles
💡 WHY THIS LAYOUT?

EFI at 512 MiB — UEFI firmware reads the ESP to find bootloaders. 512 MiB gives room for multiple kernels (linux, linux-lts, linux-zen) plus their initramfs images and microcode bundles. Some guides suggest 256 MiB, but running out of space here causes frustrating boot failures.

Swap at 8 GiB — matches or slightly exceeds your RAM, enabling hibernation (suspend-to-disk). The kernel writes the entire RAM contents to swap, powers off, and restores from swap on next boot. Without enough swap space, hibernation fails silently.

Root at 60 GiB — base system ~2 GB, desktop environment ~1 GB, dev tools + security tools can easily reach 20-30 GB, plus Docker images. 60 GB gives breathing room.

Home = remainder — your projects, downloads, configs, VMs, everything personal. Separating /home from / means you can reinstall Arch without losing your data — just format / and remount /home.

4.2 Partitioning with fdisk

Preview — Interactive fdisk session creates 4 partitions on the blank SSD. Takes ~2 minutes.
ΔDT — Before: clean disk, no partition table. After: GPT with 4 partitions (EFI, swap, root, home), no filesystems yet.
bash 
# Launch fdisk on your NVMe drive
fdisk /dev/nvme0n1

# ─── Inside fdisk interactive mode ───
# Create a new GPT partition table
Command: g

# ── Partition 1: EFI System Partition (512 MiB) ──
Command: n
Partition number: 1       # (press Enter for default)
First sector: (Enter)      # accept default (2048)
Last sector: +512M        # 512 MiB from start

# Change type to EFI System
Command: t
Partition type: 1         # 1 = EFI System

# ── Partition 2: Swap (8 GiB) ──
Command: n
Partition number: 2
First sector: (Enter)
Last sector: +8G

# Change type to Linux swap
Command: t
Partition number: 2
Partition type: 19        # 19 = Linux swap

# ── Partition 3: Root (60 GiB) ──
Command: n
Partition number: 3
First sector: (Enter)
Last sector: +60G

# ── Partition 4: Home (rest of disk) ──
Command: n
Partition number: 4
First sector: (Enter)
Last sector: (Enter)      # uses all remaining space

# ── Verify the layout ──
Command: p

# ── Write to disk and exit ──
Command: w
Expected Output (from 'p' command)
Device             Start       End   Sectors   Size Type
/dev/nvme0n1p1      2048   1050623   1048576   512M EFI System
/dev/nvme0n1p2   1050624  17827839  16777216     8G Linux swap
/dev/nvme0n1p3  17827840 143656959 125829120    60G Linux filesystem
/dev/nvme0n1p4 143656960 500118158 356461199 169.9G Linux filesystem

What just happened at the hardware level: The g command wrote a fresh GPT: a protective MBR at LBA 0 (for legacy compatibility), the primary GPT header at LBA 1, and 128 partition entries starting at LBA 2. Each n command populated one of those 128-byte partition entry slots with the start LBA, end LBA, type GUID, and a random unique partition GUID generated from /dev/urandom. The t command changed the type GUID — for EFI, it set C12A7328-F81F-11D2-BA4B-00A0C93EC93B, which the UEFI firmware uses to identify bootable partitions. The w command wrote the primary GPT to the start of the disk and the backup GPT to the last sectors, recalculated CRC32 checksums for both, and issued a kernel ioctl (BLKRRPART) to make the kernel re-read the partition table without a reboot.

4.3 Creating Filesystems

Preview — Format each partition with the appropriate filesystem. Takes ~5 seconds each.
ΔDT — Before: raw partitions with no filesystem metadata. After: each partition has a formatted filesystem ready to store files.
bash 
# EFI System Partition — MUST be FAT32 (UEFI spec requires it)
mkfs.fat -F 32 /dev/nvme0n1p1

# Swap partition
mkswap /dev/nvme0n1p2

# Root partition — ext4 is battle-tested and fast
mkfs.ext4 /dev/nvme0n1p3

# Home partition
mkfs.ext4 /dev/nvme0n1p4
Expected Output
mkfs.fat 4.2 (2021-01-31)
Setting up swapspace version 1, size = 8 GiB
Creating filesystem with 15728640 4k blocks and 3932160 inodes
Creating filesystem with 44557649 4k blocks and 11141120 inodes

What mkfs.ext4 does internally: It divides the partition into block groups (~128 MB each). Each block group gets: a superblock copy (filesystem metadata — total blocks, free blocks, inode count, mount count, timestamps), a group descriptor table, a block bitmap (one bit per 4 KB block indicating used/free), an inode bitmap (one bit per inode), an inode table (256-byte entries for each inode containing permissions, timestamps, pointers to data blocks), and the actual data blocks. The root inode (inode 2) is created as an empty directory. The journal (a dedicated area for write-ahead logging) is allocated — before any metadata change is committed to the filesystem, it is first written to the journal so that in case of a crash, fsck can replay the journal to restore consistency rather than scanning the entire filesystem.

4.4 Mounting the Hierarchy

bash 
# Mount root first — this is the base of our new filesystem tree
mount /dev/nvme0n1p3 /mnt

# Create mount points for the other partitions
mkdir -p /mnt/boot
mkdir -p /mnt/home

# Mount EFI partition at /boot
mount /dev/nvme0n1p1 /mnt/boot

# Mount home partition
mount /dev/nvme0n1p4 /mnt/home

# Activate swap
swapon /dev/nvme0n1p2

# Verify everything is mounted correctly
lsblk
Expected Output
NAME          MAJ:MIN RM   SIZE RO TYPE MOUNTPOINTS
nvme0n1       259:0    0 238.5G  0 disk
├─nvme0n1p1   259:1    0   512M  0 part /mnt/boot
├─nvme0n1p2   259:2    0     8G  0 part [SWAP]
├─nvme0n1p3   259:3    0    60G  0 part /mnt
└─nvme0n1p4   259:4    0 169.9G  0 part /mnt/home

Mounting creates a mapping in the kernel's VFS (Virtual Filesystem Switch) layer. When you mount /dev/nvme0n1p3 /mnt, the kernel reads the ext4 superblock from the partition, initializes the in-memory filesystem data structures, and associates the path /mnt with this filesystem instance. Any file access under /mnt now traverses the ext4 code paths. The order matters: root (/mnt) must be mounted first because /mnt/boot is a directory within the root filesystem — it needs to exist before we can mount the ESP on top of it.

↑ Back to top
Chapter 5

Base System Installation

★ ★ ★ ★ ★
📝 Notes

5.1 Mirrors & pacstrap

Layman version: pacstrap is the delivery truck that brings all the core packages from the internet and installs them onto your mounted partitions. Before dispatching the truck, we tell it which warehouse (mirror server) to use — ideally one geographically close to you for maximum speed.

Technical detail: pacstrap is a shell script wrapper around pacman that bootstraps a new root filesystem. It bind-mounts the live environment's package cache into the target, initializes pacman's database at /mnt/var/lib/pacman, imports the Arch Linux signing keys, and then runs pacman -Sy --root /mnt to install the specified packages with full dependency resolution.

bash 
# Optional: Update the mirror list for faster downloads
# reflector automatically ranks mirrors by speed and freshness
reflector --country Germany --age 12 --protocol https --sort rate --save /etc/pacman.d/mirrorlist

# Install the base system — this is the big one!
# base          = minimal Arch (systemd, pacman, bash, coreutils)
# linux         = the Linux kernel
# linux-firmware= firmware blobs for hardware (WiFi, GPU, etc.)
# base-devel    = compilers, make, etc. (needed for AUR later)
# intel-ucode   = Intel CPU microcode patches (critical for security)
# vim nano      = text editors (you'll need at least one!)
# networkmanager= handles networking after reboot
# git           = you're a developer, you need this immediately
pacstrap -K /mnt \
  base linux linux-firmware base-devel \
  intel-ucode \
  sof-firmware                     # Intel SOF audio (Dell 7420 REQUIRES this) \
  vim nano \
  networkmanager \
  git
Expected Output (abbreviated)
:: Synchronizing package databases...
 core             ...
 extra            ...
:: There are 50 members in group base-devel:
...
resolving dependencies...
looking for conflicting packages...
Packages (180)  base-2-2  linux-6.x-arch1  linux-firmware-20250xxx  ...
Total Download Size:    ~700 MiB
Total Installed Size:  ~2500 MiB
:: Proceed with installation? [Y/n] Y
...
(downloads and installs ~180 packages — takes 2-15 minutes depending on internet speed)

The -K flag tells pacstrap to initialize a new pacman keyring in the target system rather than copying the live environment's keyring. This is important because the target system needs its own set of trusted GPG keys for future package verification. The base meta-package pulls in approximately 50 packages forming the minimal viable Arch installation: glibc (the C library), systemd (init and service manager), bash (shell), coreutils (ls, cp, mv, etc.), util-linux (mount, fdisk, etc.), pacman (package manager), and their dependencies. The linux package is the actual kernel image and its modules — without it, your system cannot boot. The linux-firmware package contains binary firmware blobs (proprietary code that runs on your hardware's embedded processors — WiFi chips, GPU controllers, etc.).

5.2 Generating fstab

Preview — Creates the /etc/fstab file that tells the system which partitions to mount and where, every time it boots.
bash 
# Generate fstab using UUIDs (more reliable than device names like /dev/nvme0n1p3
# because device names can change if you add/remove drives)
genfstab -U /mnt >> /mnt/etc/fstab

# ALWAYS verify the generated fstab!
cat /mnt/etc/fstab
Expected Output
# /dev/nvme0n1p3
UUID=a1b2c3d4-...  /       ext4   rw,relatime    0 1

# /dev/nvme0n1p1
UUID=ABCD-1234     /boot   vfat   rw,relatime,...  0 2

# /dev/nvme0n1p4
UUID=e5f6g7h8-...  /home   ext4   rw,relatime    0 2

# /dev/nvme0n1p2
UUID=i9j0k1l2-...  none    swap   defaults       0 0

The genfstab script examines the current mount table (/proc/mounts) and generates an fstab entry for each mounted partition under /mnt. The -U flag uses UUIDs instead of device paths. UUIDs are 128-bit identifiers embedded in the filesystem superblock during formatting — they are globally unique and tied to the filesystem, not to the device enumeration order. This means if you add a second drive later, your root partition's UUID remains the same even if its device name changes from nvme0n1p3 to something else. The last two columns in fstab are dump (0 = don't use dump for backups, nearly always 0) and pass (filesystem check order: 1 for root, 2 for others, 0 for skip).

5.3 Entering the New System (arch-chroot)

bash 
# chroot = "change root" — makes /mnt appear as / for all subsequent commands
# arch-chroot is an enhanced wrapper that also bind-mounts /proc, /sys, /dev
arch-chroot /mnt
Expected Output
[root@archiso /]#

Layman version: Imagine you have been standing outside your new house looking in through the window while installing furniture. arch-chroot is the moment you step inside and close the door — now you are inside the new system. Every command you run from this point on affects the installed system, not the USB live environment.

Technical detail: The chroot system call changes the apparent root directory for the current process and all its children. After chroot /mnt, any path resolution starting with / goes to what was previously /mnt. The arch-chroot wrapper first bind-mounts the kernel virtual filesystems: /proc (process information pseudo-filesystem), /sys (sysfs — device/driver tree), /dev (device nodes), and /run (runtime data). These are necessary because many system tools (systemctl, pacman, mkinitcpio) need to communicate with the running kernel through these interfaces. Without them, you would get errors like "Failed to connect to bus" or "No such file or directory" for /dev/null.

↑ Back to top
Chapter 6

System Configuration

★ ★ ★ ★ ★
📝 Notes

6.1 Timezone & Locale

bash 
# Set timezone — creates a symlink from /etc/localtime to the zoneinfo file
ln -sf /usr/share/zoneinfo/Europe/Berlin /etc/localtime

# Sync hardware clock (RTC) to system clock
hwclock --systohc

# Configure locale — edit /etc/locale.gen and uncomment your locales
# For German + English (recommended to have both):
sed -i 's/#en_US.UTF-8/en_US.UTF-8/' /etc/locale.gen
sed -i 's/#de_DE.UTF-8/de_DE.UTF-8/' /etc/locale.gen

# Generate locales
locale-gen

# Set the system language
echo "LANG=en_US.UTF-8" > /etc/locale.conf

# Set the console keyboard layout permanently
echo "KEYMAP=de-latin1" > /etc/vconsole.conf
Expected Output (from locale-gen)
Generating locales...
  en_US.UTF-8... done
  de_DE.UTF-8... done
Generation complete.

The timezone symlink tells the C library (glibc) where to find timezone rules. When any program calls localtime(), glibc reads /etc/localtime (a binary TZif file) to convert UTC timestamps to local time. The hwclock --systohc writes the current system time to the hardware Real-Time Clock (RTC), a battery-backed chip on the motherboard that keeps time even when the system is powered off. The locale system is how Linux handles language, number formats, date formats, and character encoding. locale-gen compiles the locale definitions from source files in /usr/share/i18n into binary files in /usr/lib/locale. The LANG variable in locale.conf sets the default locale for all programs.

6.2 Hostname & Hosts

bash 
# Set your hostname — this is your machine's name on the network
echo "pinkgalaxy" > /etc/hostname

# Configure the hosts file for local resolution
cat > /etc/hosts << 'EOF'
127.0.0.1   localhost
::1         localhost
127.0.1.1   pinkgalaxy.localdomain pinkgalaxy
EOF
Expected Output
(no output — files are written silently)

The hostname is used by systemd to set the kernel hostname (visible in /proc/sys/kernel/hostname) and shown in the shell prompt. The /etc/hosts file is consulted by the C library's resolver before DNS queries — the 127.0.1.1 entry maps your hostname to a loopback address so that tools requiring hostname resolution (many network applications) work correctly even without a DNS server.

6.3 Initramfs (mkinitcpio)

Layman version: The initramfs is a tiny pre-boot operating system that loads just enough drivers to find and mount your actual root filesystem. Think of it as the scaffolding around a building under construction — it exists only to help build the structure, and is removed once the real system takes over.

bash 
# Regenerate the initramfs (usually already done by pacstrap, but good to ensure)
mkinitcpio -P
Expected Output
==> Building image from preset: /etc/mkinitcpio.d/linux.preset: 'default'
  -> -k /boot/vmlinuz-linux -c /etc/mkinitcpio.conf -g /boot/initramfs-linux.img
==> Starting build: '6.x.x-arch1-1'
  -> Running build hook: [base]
  -> Running build hook: [udev]
  -> Running build hook: [autodetect]
  -> Running build hook: [modconf]
  -> Running build hook: [kms]
  -> Running build hook: [keyboard]
  -> Running build hook: [keymap]
  -> Running build hook: [consolefont]
  -> Running build hook: [block]
  -> Running build hook: [filesystems]
  -> Running build hook: [fsck]
==> Generating module dependencies
==> Creating zstd-compressed initcpio image: '/boot/initramfs-linux.img'
==> Image generation successful
==> Building image from preset: /etc/mkinitcpio.d/linux.preset: 'fallback'
  ...
==> Image generation successful

Technical detail: mkinitcpio builds a CPIO archive (a simple file archive format) that is compressed with zstd. The "hooks" listed in the output are build scripts that determine which kernel modules, binaries, and configuration files to include. The autodetect hook is particularly clever: it examines your currently running hardware (via /sys) and includes only the modules needed for your specific devices, keeping the image small. The block hook includes storage drivers (NVMe, AHCI), filesystems includes ext4/btrfs/vfat modules, and keyboard includes USB HID and AT keyboard drivers so you can type during early boot. Two images are generated: "default" (autodetected, smaller) and "fallback" (includes all modules, for emergencies).

6.4 Root Password

bash 
# Set the root password — you MUST set this or you won't be able to log in!
passwd

# You'll be prompted to type the password twice (it won't show on screen)
Expected Output
New password:
Retype new password:
passwd: password updated successfully

The passwd command hashes your password using SHA-512 (with 5000 rounds by default) with a random salt and stores the result in /etc/shadow. The shadow file is readable only by root (mode 0640), separating password hashes from the world-readable /etc/passwd. Choose a strong password — this is the root account, which has unrestricted access to every file and process on the system.

6.5 Bootloader — systemd-boot

Layman version: The bootloader is the very first program that runs after the BIOS/UEFI. Its job is simple: find the Linux kernel on your ESP, load it into memory, and say "go." We are using systemd-boot because it is simple, fast, and maintained by the same team that makes systemd (which is already the core of our system).

Preview — After this section, your system can boot independently without the USB stick.
bash 
# Install systemd-boot to the ESP
bootctl install

# Create the loader configuration
cat > /boot/loader/loader.conf << 'EOF'
default  arch.conf
timeout  3
console-mode max
editor   no
EOF

# Get the UUID of your root partition (we need this for the boot entry)
blkid -s UUID -o value /dev/nvme0n1p3
Expected Output
Created "/boot/EFI".
Created "/boot/EFI/systemd".
Created "/boot/EFI/BOOT".
Created "/boot/loader".
Created "/boot/loader/entries".
Copied "/usr/lib/systemd/boot/efi/systemd-bootx64.efi" to "/boot/EFI/systemd/systemd-bootx64.efi".
Copied "/usr/lib/systemd/boot/efi/systemd-bootx64.efi" to "/boot/EFI/BOOT/BOOTx64.efi".
Created EFI boot entry "Linux Boot Manager".

a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890
bash 
# Create the boot entry for Arch Linux
# REPLACE the UUID below with YOUR root partition UUID from the previous command!
cat > /boot/loader/entries/arch.conf << 'EOF'
title   Arch Linux — Pink Galaxy 🌸
linux   /vmlinuz-linux
initrd  /intel-ucode.img
initrd  /initramfs-linux.img
options root=UUID=a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890 rw quiet
EOF

# Also create a fallback entry (uses the bigger initramfs with all modules)
cat > /boot/loader/entries/arch-fallback.conf << 'EOF'
title   Arch Linux — Fallback
linux   /vmlinuz-linux
initrd  /intel-ucode.img
initrd  /initramfs-linux.img
options root=UUID=a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890 rw
EOF
Expected Output
(no output — files created silently)

Technical detail: bootctl install copies the systemd-boot EFI application to two locations on the ESP: /EFI/systemd/systemd-bootx64.efi (the primary path) and /EFI/BOOT/BOOTx64.efi (the fallback path that UEFI firmware uses when no specific boot entry exists). It also creates an NVRAM boot entry via the efibootmgr interface, telling the firmware "there is a bootloader called Linux Boot Manager at this ESP." On boot, the firmware loads this EFI application, which reads /loader/loader.conf from the same ESP, finds the default entry arch.conf, and uses the UEFI Boot Services to load the kernel and initrd files into memory. The intel-ucode.img is loaded as the first initrd — it contains CPU microcode patches that the kernel applies during very early boot (before even initializing the scheduler) to fix CPU bugs and security vulnerabilities like Spectre and Meltdown.

⚠️ CRITICAL — UUID REPLACEMENT

You MUST replace a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890 in the boot entries above with your actual root partition UUID from the blkid command. If the UUID is wrong, the kernel will boot but fail to find the root filesystem, dropping you into an emergency shell. A quick way to do this in one command:

echo "options root=UUID=$(blkid -s UUID -o value /dev/nvme0n1p3) rw quiet"

↑ Back to top
Chapter 7

Post-Install Essentials

★ ★ ★ ★ ★
📝 Notes

7.1 Creating Your User & sudo

Layman version: Running everything as root (the almighty administrator) is like driving without a seatbelt — one wrong command and you wreck the entire system. We create a normal user account for daily use, then grant it sudo powers so it can temporarily elevate to root when needed (like entering a PIN to authorize a bank transfer).

bash 
# Create your user — replace 'mari' with your chosen username
# -m = create home directory (/home/mari)
# -G wheel = add to the 'wheel' group (sudo access)
# -s /bin/bash = default shell (we'll switch to zsh later)
useradd -m -G wheel -s /bin/bash mari

# Set the user password
passwd mari

# Enable sudo for the wheel group
# This uncomments the '%wheel ALL=(ALL:ALL) ALL' line in sudoers
EDITOR=vim visudo

# Inside visudo, find and uncomment this line (remove the #):
# %wheel ALL=(ALL:ALL) ALL
# Save and exit (:wq in vim)

# Alternative one-liner (if you prefer not to use visudo interactively):
sed -i 's/^# %wheel ALL=(ALL:ALL) ALL/%wheel ALL=(ALL:ALL) ALL/' /etc/sudoers
Expected Output
New password:
Retype new password:
passwd: password updated successfully

Technical detail: The useradd command creates an entry in /etc/passwd (username, UID, GID, home directory, shell), adds the password hash to /etc/shadow, creates the home directory and populates it from /etc/skel (skeleton templates like .bashrc), and updates /etc/group to add the user to the wheel group. The wheel group is traditionally the administrative group on Unix systems. The sudoers file controls who can run commands as root via sudo. The visudo tool is essential because it acquires a lock on the sudoers file and validates syntax before saving — a syntax error in sudoers can lock you out of sudo entirely.

7.2 NetworkManager

bash 
# Enable NetworkManager to start automatically on every boot
systemctl enable NetworkManager

# Also enable systemd-resolved for DNS
systemctl enable systemd-resolved
Expected Output
Created symlink /etc/systemd/system/multi-user.target.wants/NetworkManager.service → /usr/lib/systemd/system/NetworkManager.service.
Created symlink /etc/systemd/system/multi-user.target.wants/systemd-resolved.service → ...

The systemctl enable command creates a symlink in the appropriate .target.wants directory. Systemd uses target units (like runlevels) to determine which services start during boot. The multi-user.target is the default for non-graphical systems. When systemd reaches this target during boot, it starts all services linked in the .wants directory. NetworkManager will handle both Ethernet (automatic DHCP) and Wi-Fi (via nmcli or nmtui for a text UI).

7.3 SSH Server

bash 
# Install OpenSSH
pacman -S openssh

# Enable the SSH server (starts on boot)
systemctl enable sshd

# Harden SSH config — disable root login, use key-based auth later
sed -i 's/#PermitRootLogin prohibit-password/PermitRootLogin no/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
Expected Output
resolving dependencies...
Packages (1) openssh-9.x
Total Installed Size: ~5 MiB
:: Proceed? [Y/n] Y

7.4 Firewall (ufw)

bash 
# Install ufw (Uncomplicated Firewall — a friendly frontend for iptables/nftables)
pacman -S ufw

# Set default policies: deny incoming, allow outgoing
ufw default deny incoming
ufw default allow outgoing

# Allow SSH (so you can connect remotely)
ufw allow ssh

# Enable the firewall
ufw enable

# Enable on boot
systemctl enable ufw
Expected Output
Default incoming policy changed to 'deny'
Default outgoing policy changed to 'allow'
Rule added
Rule added (v6)
Firewall is active and enabled on system startup

Layman version: A firewall is like a security guard at the entrance of your building. By default, we tell the guard: "Let residents go out freely, but do not let anyone in unless they are specifically authorized." We authorize SSH (port 22) so you can connect remotely from another machine.

Technical detail: ufw translates its high-level rules into nftables (the modern Linux packet filtering framework that replaced iptables). The "deny incoming" default adds a rule to the INPUT chain that drops all packets not matching an explicit ACCEPT rule. The "allow ssh" rule adds an ACCEPT for TCP port 22 in both IPv4 and IPv6. Nftables operates in the kernel's netfilter subsystem, which hooks into specific points in the network stack (PREROUTING, INPUT, FORWARD, OUTPUT, POSTROUTING) and evaluates each packet against the rule chains.

7.5 AUR Helper — paru

Layman version: The official Arch repositories contain thousands of packages, but the community maintains even more in the AUR (Arch User Repository). paru is a helper tool that lets you install AUR packages almost as easily as regular ones.

bash 
# paru must be built from source (it's in the AUR itself!)
# First exit chroot, then re-enter as your user... OR do this after first reboot.
# For now, let's prepare git (already installed) and build paru after reboot.

# AFTER REBOOT as your normal user:
cd /tmp
git clone https://aur.archlinux.org/paru.git
cd paru
makepkg -si

# After installation, you can install AUR packages like:
# paru -S package-name
Expected Output
Cloning into 'paru'...
==> Making package: paru 2.x-1
==> Checking runtime dependencies...
==> Checking buildtime dependencies...
==> Retrieving sources...
==> Compiling...
==> Installing paru with pacman -U...
(paru is now installed and ready to use)

7.6 Essential System Packages

Before rebooting, let's install a comprehensive set of essential packages that you will need immediately:

bash 
# ── Core system utilities ──
pacman -S \
  man-db man-pages texinfo         # Manual pages \
  bash-completion                   # Tab completion for bash \
  reflector                         # Mirror ranking tool \
  pacman-contrib                    # paccache, checkupdates, etc. \
  htop btop                         # Process monitors \
  neofetch fastfetch                # System info display \
  tree                              # Directory structure viewer \
  wget curl                         # HTTP clients \
  unzip p7zip                       # Archive tools \
  rsync                             # File synchronization \
  fzf                               # Fuzzy finder (game changer!) \
  ripgrep fd bat                    # Modern replacements for grep/find/cat \
  eza                               # Modern ls replacement \
  zoxide                            # Smarter cd \
  tmux                              # Terminal multiplexer \
  less                              # Pager \
  jq                                # JSON processor \
  bc                                # Calculator \
  dosfstools exfatprogs ntfs-3g     # Filesystem tools for USB drives \
  smartmontools                     # SSD health monitoring \
  lm_sensors                        # Hardware temperature sensors
Expected Output
resolving dependencies...
Packages (30+) ...
Total Download Size: ~50 MiB
Total Installed Size: ~180 MiB
:: Proceed? [Y/n] Y

7.7 Reboot into Your New System

🌸 THE MOMENT OF TRUTH

You are about to exit the chroot, unmount everything, remove the USB stick, and boot into your freshly installed Arch Linux. If everything was configured correctly, you will see the systemd-boot menu, select "Arch Linux — Pink Galaxy 🌸", and land at a login prompt. This is the most satisfying moment of any Arch install.

bash 
# Exit the chroot environment
exit

# Unmount all partitions recursively
umount -R /mnt

# Turn off swap
swapoff /dev/nvme0n1p2

# Reboot — REMOVE THE USB STICK when the screen goes black!
reboot
Expected Output (after reboot)
Arch Linux — Pink Galaxy 🌸      ← systemd-boot menu
Arch Linux — Fallback

(select first entry, press Enter)

pinkgalaxy login: mari
Password: ********
[mari@pinkgalaxy ~]$              ← YOU ARE IN! 🎉

If the system boots successfully and you can log in, congratulations — you have installed Arch Linux from scratch! The base system is now running. In Part 2, we will transform this bare console into a stunning pink galaxy developer environment with Hyprland (Wayland compositor), security tools, and a full rice.

🔧 TROUBLESHOOTING — IF IT DOESN'T BOOT

Black screen, no boot menu: The UEFI firmware cannot find the bootloader. Re-enter the live USB, mount your partitions, chroot in, and verify /boot/EFI/BOOT/BOOTx64.efi exists. Re-run bootctl install.

Boot menu appears but kernel panics: The root UUID in your boot entry is wrong. Boot from USB, mount partitions, chroot, and fix the UUID in /boot/loader/entries/arch.conf.

"Failed to mount root": Same as above — UUID mismatch. Or the initramfs is missing the necessary drivers. Try the fallback entry.

No network after reboot: Did you systemctl enable NetworkManager? Log in as root, run systemctl start NetworkManager and systemctl enable NetworkManager.

↑ Back to top

References

Arch Wiki — Installation Guide: wiki.archlinux.org/title/Installation_guide — The canonical source of truth for every step in this guide.

Arch Wiki — General Recommendations: wiki.archlinux.org/title/General_recommendations — Post-installation best practices.

Arch Wiki — systemd-boot: wiki.archlinux.org/title/Systemd-boot

Arch Wiki — Network Configuration: wiki.archlinux.org/title/Network_configuration

Arch Wiki — Security: wiki.archlinux.org/title/Security

Arch Wiki — Solid State Drive: wiki.archlinux.org/title/Solid_state_drive

NVMe CLI documentation: github.com/linux-nvme/nvme-cli

Dell Inspiron BIOS settings: dell.com/support (search your specific model for BIOS documentation)

Planner Z.0 — Continuation (DO NOT INDEX)

Missing Topics for Part 2

#AnchorTopicObjective
1ch8-displayDisplay Server & CompositorXorg vs Wayland; install Hyprland as the compositor/WM
2ch9-terminalTerminal & ShellKitty/Alacritty terminal; Zsh + Oh-My-Zsh + Powerlevel10k; tmux config
3ch10-audioAudio, Bluetooth & PeripheralsPipeWire + WirePlumber; Bluetooth (bluez); CUPS printing
4ch11-devDeveloper EnvironmentPython (pyenv/poetry), Node (nvm), Rust, Docker, VS Code, Neovim, PostgreSQL, Redis, AWS CLI
5ch12-securityWhite Hat Security Arsenalnmap, Wireshark, Burp Suite, Metasploit, John, Hashcat, aircrack-ng, Ghidra, sqlmap, gobuster, OWASP ZAP
6ch13-ricePink Galaxy RiceHyprland config, Waybar, Rofi, color scheme, wallpapers, GTK/Qt theming, terminal colors
7ch14-maintSystem Maintenance & HardeningPacman maintenance, backup (timeshift/borg), hardening checklist, AppArmor

Next Prompts Pack

PROMPT 1 — Part 2: Display, Terminal & Audio
INSERT_AT: ch8-display
GOALS:
- Install and configure Hyprland (Wayland compositor)
- Configure Kitty terminal with pink galaxy theme
- Set up Zsh + Oh-My-Zsh + Powerlevel10k
- PipeWire audio + Bluetooth
CALLOUTS: ENV, TIP, GOTCHA, PERFORMANCE NOTE
TARGET_FILE: archlinux_guide_part2.html
PAGE_BUDGET: ~60 pages
DEPENDENCIES: Part 1 complete (ch1-ch7)
PROMPT 2 — Part 3: Developer Environment
INSERT_AT: ch11-dev
GOALS:
- Python ecosystem (pyenv, poetry, virtualenvs)
- Node.js ecosystem (nvm, npm, yarn)
- Rust toolchain
- Docker & Docker Compose
- VS Code + extensions for Python/security
- Neovim with LazyVim
- PostgreSQL + Redis
- AWS CLI + cloud tools
CALLOUTS: ENV, TIP, GOTCHA, PERF, SIM
TARGET_FILE: archlinux_guide_part3.html
PAGE_BUDGET: ~80 pages
DEPENDENCIES: Part 1 + Part 2
PROMPT 3 — Part 4: Security Arsenal & Pink Galaxy Rice
INSERT_AT: ch12-security, ch13-rice
GOALS:
- Full white hat toolkit (nmap, wireshark, metasploit, burpsuite, etc.)
- Forensics tools (volatility, autopsy)
- CTF setup
- Complete Hyprland rice (pink/galaxy theme)
- Waybar, Rofi, wallpapers, GTK/Qt themes
- System hardening & maintenance
CALLOUTS: ATTENTION, GOTCHA, TIP, ENV
TARGET_FILE: archlinux_guide_part4.html
PAGE_BUDGET: ~80 pages
DEPENDENCIES: Parts 1-3
